Okay, if you flip to your first page, there's 97 more quick points I'm going to go through, but if you flip to your first page, this is perhaps the greatest example that I've ever seen of the importance of copy. And if any of you have heard me use this in a seminar before, don't spoil it for the rest in the room. This is an actual headline that ran in tabloid advertising for a Learn to Play the Piano course back in the 1950s. And this headline was used for many months and was successful. And in one issue of one of the magazines where it was running, a mistake was made. And there was one change made to this headline and the response to the advertising almost tripled. The question is, can you tell me what the change was? One change. I remember from the new class. I don't remember what it was. You think it's your last word? I think it was the word put. No, no, you're on the wrong page, John. Where? There. Put music in your life. There you go. It was one word? One change. One change, like a letter change? It was actually a letter change, as a matter of fact. I think they made it. Maybe it was a wife or something? Not in our memory now. Why? The old headline was put music in your life. The accidental change was puts music in your life. Put? Added an S to the word put. Now, once you've done it, if you understand human behavior, you get it immediately. The old way implies work. The new way implies that it's done for you. That's how sensitive what we do is that you can fool around and change, not just even one word, almost triple. Not just one word, but one little letter can make, obviously, the difference between profit and loss, small profits, huge profits. The next page, I think is the, and this is preaching you to convert it, because everybody in this room already knows it, I think it's the single most important thing to understand about really all print media, although this says direct mail, and that is that it is not advertising. It is what Halbert coined, I think, as salesmanship in print. And when people approach it from a comparative background to traditional advertising, it's very difficult for somebody with a traditional advertising background to shift into what they do, because they're still trying to move from the foundation of doing advertising. It's, incidentally, easier for a guy who's been a door-to-door salesperson to learn this than it is for somebody with an advertising background, because what we do is closer to door-to-door selling than it is to this. I've often told people in the infomercial, because if you have a choice between getting Steven Spielberg to produce your infomercial or a 53-year-old vacuum cleaner sales guy with gravy stains on his tie and all of the polish worn off his right toe, get the vacuum cleaner guy, because your odds are going to be better. And what you want to keep in mind whenever you're doing print is that you're making a sales call in the interview, and so all the fundamentals of selling apply, almost none of the fundamentals of advertising apply. This next headline was also in my newsletter, and it was actually from a news article, and it brings us to your national inquire or question, Chad. This happens to be true, by the way. This was in a newspaper in Canada, but it's about this, it's a whole article about this gal who used to follow all the rock stars around and make plaster casts of the lead singers, penises. And she had this huge collection of famous rock stars, penises. And there's a big lawsuit now. I can't even remember what her name was. But anyway, I've said it a coffee shop before, 7R in Vancouver, I think, and I'm skimming through Toronto, and I'm skimming through the Canadian newspaper, you know, and really just about nothing is of any interest to me. The local government stuff I could care less about, the local sports stuff I could care less about. And I mean, this headline, you know, just, I mean, huh? You know, I mean, I sat there and read this article, you know, because you just can't, I mean, you can't not read this article. And that's the whole point of the National Inquire, and the other tabloids, and the cover on Cosmopolitan. The whole point is to create headlines that you cannot not read the article. That are outrageous, but compelling. You know, you were mentioning about how he never got the point of this, and I haven't either. Every time I had to say, well, I've opened up the inquiries, I'm trying to analyze what it is. It's not how they put it together. It's not the formula to the fact that it's wow. What, it isn't so outrageous, wow. Absolutely. I mean, their job, when you realize, I mean, their subscription base, I don't know what it is, but it's certainly got to be a wow. I mean, the bulk of these things are sold off, that supermarket checkout stand on impulse. And these headlines have got to do the job. I mean, they've got to make that person pick it up and want to look inside and want to know. I mean, the Jody Inquire in mind just want to know, you know. And so, I mean, this has just got to do it. And so, as you skim through here, you see rock star, genitalia type headlines. I mean, that's what you see. And, I mean, when you stop to think about it, I mean, there is, don't think, says the largest circulation of any paper in America. And the stat I saw some years ago that I quote all the time is, there's more copies of one week of this book than the daily newspaper in the ten largest cities in America added together for the entire week. I mean, America's reading this thing, and it's not all Roseanne Barr type housewives reading it either. I mean, in fact, I mean, you know, these things are stacked up to here in the airport newsstands. Well, who's buying them in the airport newsstands? Us, are buying them in the airport newsstands. There aren't that many housewives running around on airplanes during the week, you know. I mean, when you walk in on a Monday morning in LAX and go into the newsstand and see 3,000 of these stacked up in the, you know, next to the normal newspaper, it's business guys that are buying it. So, their demographic is all over the place. And it's totally on the strength of how they grab attention and make you just, just have to take the next step. And that above all else is what a great headline does. Well, that's a business as well. My, my, my advice is not to consult them. I've had a couple of really good, uh, people or whatever, people will walk in and say something to me, the person that they see it. They're into it. Absolutely. And they would ever have read it to their supermarkets. And so, you know, as you look at these, first of all, you look for style. But beyond that, some of these, obviously, we can't, there's no way for us to knock them off. But some of them, some of them, they're off. And you particularly want to pay attention to the ones that are used in the ads, as well as the ones that are used in the articles. You know, that I think in one way, shape, or form, I may be able to use, you know, in the future and knock off one way or another. Um, uh, a lot. A lot. Yeah. And of course I do, which most of you don't, but, you know, I do stuff for a lot of different products and a lot of different product categories. So, you know, I've got a swipe file on beauty. I've got a swipe file on the diet. I've got a swipe file on sex. I've got a swipe file on date. You know, here's a headline, gals hunt for rich mates in emergency rooms. Now I'm going to save that headline and I'm going to save that article because anything that has to do with dating or meeting or romance, this is going to be of interest to me when it comes time to work on that copy. Uh, how you watch TV can threaten your marriage. Tips on tuning in to happiness. Watch out couples. Watch out couples. The way you and your spouse watch TV can ruin your relationship. That's clever. Off-beat ways too. You can apply that to a lot of stuff. Off-beat ways to make money from home in your spare time. Right? Off-beat ways to conquer the fear of public speaking. Off-beat ways to lose weight without dieting. See now, that's a good...that's very useful. That's very clever. Let's see what else we got here. You know, many of the popular writers, they just work for... Is this a big part of national? About...in the tabloid business, about half their stuff from the articles come from freelancers. And half their stuff comes from staff. But the stuff that comes in from freelancers almost entirely gets rewritten. And there's people who just do the headlines. I mean, that's their job is to do the headlines. And the highest paid guy in the place, the guy who writes the front cover. Do you know the Cosmo story? The story about Cosmo magazine? Cosmo magazine was failing. And what's her name? Husband. Hell of a joint of grounds, husband. If you've ever seen on a talk show, it's a really interesting guy anyway. He's like twice an ancient. He's the guy who came up with the headline formula for the front cover. He said, you gotta change the whole front cover of this magazine. And it's gotta sell its way off the newsstand. And so we gotta write these kind of headlines. And I mean, that magazine, and gradually all the women's magazines have copied it. Even today, Reader's Digest, which is a very conservative magazine, when you see it on display in the supermarket, there's a paper wrap around it that has different headlines and article titles on it that are on the front of the actual magazine. And they tabloid them up for sale in the supermarkets. Now if you subscribe to Reader's Digest, you never see that. The one that arrives at your house doesn't have an honor. But the one that sold at the supermarket does. These top international bankers use horoscopes to invest billions. I mean, you almost gotta know, don't you? Yeah, I think you're getting it. Well, I think it's just a lot of... Back in its empty manipulatives. Well, I mean, there's a lot of reasons, you know, or more. I mean, I think we've been... Well, even manipulatives, I mean, you didn't well better be manipulative. Right, because the product is amazing. Exactly, yeah, exactly. The analogy that I used to use in a sales seminar about that was, you're out in the middle of the ocean and you're the captain of a big boat, and you see him floating on a log out in the middle of the ocean. Star, sunburn, you know, God knows how long he's been out there. And so you pull the boat up to rescue him, and it quickly becomes apparent that he's delirious. He's been out there so long, he's lost it, you know, and he don't want to be rescued. Now, do you say, well, he's got a right not to be rescued and sail away, or do you do everything you know how to do to get the guy on the boat to save him? Well, if you have any integrity, you have any humanity, you do everything there is to get on the boat to save him. So, if you're selling something that is of legitimate worth and value to the other person, then you do everything in your power to make that sail. That's the difference between positive manipulation and negative manipulation. I'm not necessarily embarrassed, but the fact that part of it is, you know, some people more than others, but I mean, we're conditioned, you know, to be embarrassed about that, you know, that this is not professional, and this is not sophisticated, and this is not. And I mean, the country club thing is very real, believe me. There are guys unlike you in the infomercial business who are embarrassed about what they do, and who, you know, who hang out with the William Morrisons of the world, and they're in the real entertainment. I mean, look, the whole awards deal, you know, the whole award, the entire awards ceremony deal that's done at National Infomercial Marketing Association, that whole thing is embedded to make a bunch of people who are embarrassed about what they do feel like it's something other than what it is. And I mean, that's what everybody needs to feel okay. God bless them. To me, it's ridiculous. And, you know, you have to realize that here's where America is. It's not, you know, and people who live only in LA or live only in Palm Springs or live only in New York, get a very, or Washington for that matter, are really out of touch with, I mean, I, several years ago, I was at Palm Springs for a Guathy Wacker beating, and Greg and I were driving across town, and we barely got time to grab lunch. And I see, like a growth of a drug store that has a lunch counter deal, and I say, let's go in there and we'll just, you know, at the lunch counter. He'd never been in one. He said, you mean he said, what is this? You know, there's a bunch of working steps sitting around, you know, and they come in there every day, and they know Marge, and Marge knows them. This was, has foreign to him as if you had dropped him into the middle of the Soviet Union. Well, here's where he spends his time. Palm Springs, Beverly Hills, and Manhattan. I said, but that's where America is. See, America is here. You know, I mean, the Roseanne Show is America, and you know, that's where we get our money. But now this headline, this will work, this kind of thing, it will work in this, you know, again, this has, this has a formula to it. There's a formula. The first half, these top international bankers, these blank, these top athletes, these top entrepreneurs, these top writers, these top actresses and models, these top, you see, then the second half is, use homo-home to get homo-home. So we got a three-part formula. These top blank, use blank to get blank. These top actresses and models use chromium to lose weight without dieting. You see it? And out that, if you took a year's worth of national inquires and you went just to find that formula, you would see that formula repeated over and over and over again. The reason that these credible people are doing this. I'm like me, average American people that have to respect our getting results by doing this thing. That's right. I'm, I'm, I'm, imagine this, I mean, would you read this, this article? These former Fortune 500 CEOs and former U.S. presidents use this little known system to make big money from home. Yeah, I agree with that. Why? Because I just gave you people who are, who are more credible, who are more credible than you, you know, in essence, without being insulting, and hooked them up to something you want to do. Should we do anything with that? Well, what's, what's the eye catcher? Mystery. Right. Mystery experts. Mystery Wall Street bankers, secrets, could mystery. New surgery cures painful heel spurs in days. Seeing the fact that they put the word simple in there and led with it makes a big difference, doesn't it? Yeah. You know, they have slick covers, but they have blueprint guts. Entrepreneur and success, and those are a great above these. But these are like the tabloids of the buddy making business. And you will find, you know, a similar headline techniques. And, you know, I've got two ads now running and these are both doing very well. And the headlines are both... No, you can't. Yeah, you can, but he's probably got two subscribers. This is what's called control surf. And these magazines, basically what they do is they rent lists of opportunity seekers. And they mail like one third to the same list, two or three issues in a row, and two thirds to new names every month. And so it's really co-op advertising more than it is a real magazine, but it's highly effective. They've got some advertisers that have been with them for 50 years. Don Perry puts this magazine out and this thing is old in the hills. I don't know how long it's been around. The number half of the venture is still a little... Bankrupt. Okay, your next page. A couple things about headliners. First of all, as you probably know and why we're spending so much time on, most people who do a lot of direct response will tell you that the headline is somewhere between 70 and 80% of the battle. During the brief time that I had a regular agency, I would have copywriters come to me with a headline that made no sense. And the response always was, would you give me a shot of one? And the response always was, yeah, but if you read the article, the headline's really cute. Read the ad, the headline is really cute. I was hoping I'll like it. The last time I asked you, you could have won it. It worked, I guess I should have specified in the last... Oh, oh, oh. Slow to this. And see, that's just wrong. The headline has to work or really nothing else matters. And a guy like Ted Nicholas will tell you he spends days getting the headline right before he worries about much else. This is the end of Side 1. Please turn the cassette over for Side 2.
